label: delphi-r1-p1
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

In the City of Rio Robles (invented), Councilmember Dana Okafor's spouse is the salaried Director of Programs (~$95,000/yr) at Harbor Light Alliance, a 501(c)(3) homeless-services provider. Under a 2019 city resolution, Okafor also sits on Harbor Light's board of directors, uncompensated, as the council's designated liaison.

The council is about to (a) adopt the framework and scoring criteria for a competitive RFP allocating $2.3M in combined city general-fund and state HHAP pass-through money for homelessness services, and then (b) award the resulting contract(s). Harbor Light is one of three realistic bidders and has historically won about 60% of the city's homelessness contracts. The spouse's position is funded out of Harbor Light's general operating budget, to which city contracts contribute roughly 40%.

Okafor asks the city attorney: May I vote on the RFP framework? On the award? Must I resign the board seat or must my spouse's employment change? And if I simply recuse, is the city safe contracting with Harbor Light?

## The question

Analyze under Government Code § 1090 (including whether board membership plus a spouse's salaried employment is a prohibited financial interest 'in' the contract, whether any § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies, and whether recusal even cures § 1090 for a board member), and under the Political Reform Act (§§ 87100, 87103 — community-property interest in spouse's income, materiality, and the public-generally exception), plus common-law conflict doctrine. What combination of resignation, recusal, wall procedures, or contract structuring lets the city lawfully fund Harbor Light, if any? What are the consequences of getting it wrong (§ 1090 voidness, disgorgement, criminal exposure, FPPC enforcement)?

## Proposition to assess

"If the city awards the homelessness-services contract to Harbor Light Alliance while Okafor holds the board seat and her spouse holds her position — even with Okafor fully recused from the RFP and the award — the contract would be void or voidable under Government Code § 1090."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the text of the governing statute or constitutional provision, read closely.
text: {"probability":78,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not TRUE: on these facts, Okafor likely holds a § 1090 \"financial interest\" in the Harbor Light contract (through her spouse's management-level, substantially city-funded salary, and/or her own board seat), and because she sits on the very body awarding the contract, well-settled case law holds that her recusal does not cure the defect unless a specific § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies and is properly invoked. Given the seniority of the spouse's role and the contractor's heavy dependence on city funding, this interest looks too substantial to fit comfortably within the narrow, disclosure-based exceptions, so the contract would likely be void under § 1092 despite full recusal — though the exact statutory fit is genuinely uncertain enough to keep this below near-certainty.","rationales":["Under Thomson v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289) tends to treat as disqualifying rather than remote.","Okafor's own uncompensated board seat may independently be shielded by the § 1091.5(a)(8) noninterest exception for a noncompensated nonprofit officer, but that carve-out does not obviously extend to the separate and more consequential interest created by her spouse's compensated employment.","Because § 1090 voids the contract itself under § 1092 once an unexempted interested member sits on the awarding body, the remedy is structural rather than procedural, so 'full recusal' as framed in the proposition is legally insufficient by design if no exception applies (see also Lexin v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633","Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565","People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289","Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050","California Attorney General opinions addressing spousal employment with a contracting party as a § 1090 financial interest (specific opinion number to be verified)","Gov. Code §§ 87100, 87103, 87103(c) (Political Reform Act — cited for contrast; not itself dispositive of § 1090 voidness)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether the spouse's Director-of-Programs position and Harbor Light's funding structure fall within (or squarely outside) any enumerated § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception — that single statutory-fit question is outcome-determinative, and I lack full confidence in the precise current text of § 1091(b)'s spousal-employment-related subsections and how a court would apply them to a senior, policy-adjacent role rather than a rank-and-file job."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not TRUE: on these facts, Okafor likely holds a § 1090 "financial interest" in the Harbor Light contract (through her spouse's management-level, substantially city-funded salary, and/or her own board seat), and because she sits on the very body awarding the contract, well-settled case law holds that her recusal does not cure the defect unless a specific § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies and is properly invoked. Given the seniority of the spouse's role and the contractor's heavy dependence on city funding, this interest looks too substantial to fit comfortably within the narrow, disclosure-based exceptions, so the contract would likely be void under § 1092 despite full recusal — though the exact statutory fit is genuinely uncertain enough to keep this below near-certainty.
rationales: Under Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 and Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, recusal/abstention by an interested member of the very body making the contract does not cure a § 1090 violation, because the statute's prohibition runs to the body's composition, not merely to who casts the vote, absent a fitting statutory exception.

A spouse's salaried, program-director-level (i.e., management/policy-adjacent) position at a nonprofit whose operating budget is ~40% city-contract-funded, and which wins ~60% of the relevant city contracts, is the kind of non-speculative, substantial interest that § 1090's broad case-law construction (e.g., People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289) tends to treat as disqualifying rather than remote.

Okafor's own uncompensated board seat may independently be shielded by the § 1091.5(a)(8) noninterest exception for a noncompensated nonprofit officer, but that carve-out does not obviously extend to the separate and more consequential interest created by her spouse's compensated employment.

Because § 1090 voids the contract itself under § 1092 once an unexempted interested member sits on the awarding body, the remedy is structural rather than procedural, so 'full recusal' as framed in the proposition is legally insufficient by design if no exception applies (see also Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050 on the breadth of disqualifying interests and limits of curing by non-participation).

The main source of doubt is whether a specific § 1091(b) 'remote interest' category for spousal employment (if one squarely exists and is properly invoked with disclosure) could apply to a Director-of-Programs-level position, which — if it does — would flip the analysis and make the proposition false; I am not fully certain of that subsection's precise text and fit to these facts.
authorities: Gov. Code § 1091 (remote interests, esp. any spousal-employment subsection — verify exact paragraph)
Gov. Code § 1091.5(a)(8) (noninterest exception for noncompensated nonprofit officer — verify exact text/conditions)
Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565
California Attorney General opinions addressing spousal employment with a contracting party as a § 1090 financial interest (specific opinion number to be verified)
Gov. Code §§ 87100, 87103, 87103(c) (Political Reform Act — cited for contrast; not itself dispositive of § 1090 voidness)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the spouse's Director-of-Programs position and Harbor Light's funding structure fall within (or squarely outside) any enumerated § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception — that single statutory-fit question is outcome-determinative, and I lack full confidence in the precise current text of § 1091(b)'s spousal-employment-related subsections and how a court would apply them to a senior, policy-adjacent role rather than a rank-and-file job.
